draft.introduction: Iliad 22 narrates the death of Hector at the hands of Achilles before the walls of Troy. The pursuit, deception by Athena, and Hector’s final stand constitute the tragic climax of the entire poem.

translation.text: Hector spoke to Achilles: ‘I will no longer flee you. Three times I ran around the city of Priam, and dared not stand against you. But now my spirit bids me face you. Let me not die without a struggle and without glory, but in some great deed of which men yet to be born shall hear.’
translation.original_text: μή σε γέρον κεφαλήν γουνάζομαι οὐδ’ ἐλεαίρεις

translator_notes.note: Hector’s decision to stand and fight after fleeing three times around Troy enacts the Homeric ideal of kleos (glory). Knowing he will die, he chooses how to die—the only freedom left to him. This is the archetype of tragic heroism in Western literature.
